coredata.dc:title: Low-energy-consumption rapid synthesis of carbon dots at room temperature from combusted food waste with versatile analytical applications
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Jun Yi
Huang, Yi Chen
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we developed a low-energy-consumption green method for synthesising carbon dots (CD) at room temperature using watermelon rind as the carbon source through a cutting process based on NaCl crystals. The synthesis process was rapid (<5 min) and facile. The synthesised CD were characterised using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, energy-dispersive X-ray analysis and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The results revealed that the synthesised dots exhibited robust adsorption, a spherical shape and a uniform size distribution. The PL intensity of the quantum dots decrease due to the temperature rising, metal ions and ponceau 4R added. In contrast with PH, PL intensity increase, so these CD can serve as multifunctional sensing materials. Overall, this study presents an environmentally friendly method for the rapid synthesis of CD that are suitable for temperature, pH, metal ion and food sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green method
Rapid
Carbon dots
Food waste

Taiwan Ministry of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) of Taiwan ( MOST 111-2222-E-231-001- ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed synthesis method: (a) preparation of carbon source, (b) production of CD at room temperature.
PL excitation and emission spectra of CD.

Influence of various factors on fluorescence emission spectra of CDs: (a) temperature (25 °C: black line; 100 °C: red line), (b) added Fe3+ metal ions (without Fe3+: black line; with Fe3+: red line), (c) pH (PH7: black line; PH14: red line), (d) time, and (e) PL analysis of real sample (carbonate beverages) containing ponceau 4R.
SEM and EDX analyses of CD: (a) SEM image of CD at 50,000× magnification, (b) SEM image of CD at 100,000× magnification, (c) EDX spectrum of CD, (d) Elemental mapping of CD.
TEM image of carbon quantum dots.
XPS patterns of carbon quantum dots: (a) full-scan spectrum, (b) C1s zone, (c) O1s zone (d) XRD spectrum of CD.

Commercially available luminance metre used to sense brightness changes of carbon quantum dot solution: (a) blank, (b) temperature, (c) Fe3+, (d) pH, (e) real food (carbonate beverages) containing ponceau 4R, (f) irradiation and measurement directions.

head.ce:title: Low-energy-consumption rapid synthesis of carbon dots at room temperature from combusted food waste with versatile analytical applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science, National Taiwan Ocean University, Keelung 202301, Taiwan, ROC
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science, National Taiwan Ocean University, Keelung 202301, Taiwan, ROC

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we developed a low-energy-consumption green method for synthesising carbon dots (CD) at room temperature using watermelon rind as the carbon source through a cutting process based on NaCl crystals. The synthesis process was rapid (<5 min) and facile. The synthesised CD were characterised using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, energy-dispersive X-ray analysis and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The results revealed that the synthesised dots exhibited robust adsorption, a spherical shape and a uniform size distribution. The PL intensity of the quantum dots decrease due to the temperature rising, metal ions and ponceau 4R added. In contrast with PH, PL intensity increase, so these CD can serve as multifunctional sensing materials. Overall, this study presents an environmentally friendly method for the rapid synthesis of CD that are suitable for temperature, pH, metal ion and food sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CD) have attracted attention in recent years, particularly in sensing (Matsuda et al., 2013; Wu et al., 2021; Ai et al., 2021; Xin et al., 2019; Yoo et al., 2019), bioimaging (Bhatt et al., 2020) and antiviral (Du et al., 2016) applications. Early methods of synthesising CD commonly involved top–down processes, where physical methods are used to destroy or peel off larger carbon material structures to form carbon quantum dots. However, these processes involve toxic chemicals (Liu et al., 2019) and consume considerable energy and time (Liu et al., 2019). Therefore, CD synthesis methods that use nontoxic materials and low-energy-consumption rapid-synthesis processes should be developed.

Fruit waste often becomes a breeding ground for mosquitoes and fruit flies, engendering environmental problems. Hence, recycling such waste is crucial for solving these environmental problems. Many food items are highly perishable and need temperature control to remain fresh and avoid spoilage, as high temperatures accelerate the growth of bacteria and microorganisms. Moreover, pH and metal ions influence the behaviour of biological systems. Therefore, developing effective pH, metal ion and temperature sensors (Matsuda et al., 2013; Wu et al., 2021; Xin et al., 2019) based on materials prepared from recycled food waste is valuable. Accordingly, we developed a green method for synthesising CD that can be used in temperature sensors. This method involves using insoluble NaCl crystals to transform food waste into CD at 1 atm and 25 °C. Therefore, the proposed method not only addresses the environmental problems associated with food waste but also functions as a low-energy, simple, green approach to CD synthesis. We characterised the synthesised CD using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS).

We utilised burned and carbonised watermelon rind as the carbon source for CD synthesis, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). Specifically, 0.5 g of the carbon source was added to a 10 mL water solution containing 10 g of NaCl. This mixture was placed in a press breaker operated at 3000 rpm for 4 min; in this instrument, the NaCl crystals cut the bulk carbon through shear force produced by the press breaker, which helped convert the bulk carbon into CD at 1 atm and 25 °C, as shown in Fig. 1(b). The resulting light-yellow solution, which contained CD and trace amounts of NaCl, was then filtered using a syringe filter. Ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation revealed that the solution was blue. Fig. 1 illustrates the proposed synthesis process.

Fig. 2 displays the PL spectra of the CD. The CD exhibited strong absorption properties, appearing yellow under standard lighting and blue under UV excitation (λ = 339 nm; Fig. 2, inset). The PL emission intensity peaked at 410 nm, when the dots were excited at 339 nm. The measured PL intensity of the quantum dots was 300,000 cps; thus, they were high-intensity particles. Particle aggregation, which can cause light-induced quenching, did not occur. However, after being at room temperature for two days, the CD still had high PL intensity, indicating high quality and low defects.

Fig. 3 presents the fluorescence emission spectra of the CD at various temperatures. A rise in temperature caused the carbon quantum dots to collide and the resulting CD aggregation induced fluorescence quenching; this result is the same as that of a previous study (Matsuda et al., 2013). Non-radiative channels are not activated at room temperature, so excited electrons can radiatively emit photons (Xin et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019). In other words, a temperature rise cause more non-radiative channels to activate and excited electrons return to the ground state through non-radiative processes, thus decreasing the fluorescence intensity. A previous report (He et al., 2020) also indicated that a temperature increase enhances CD aggregation. About previous mechanism is CD fluorescence quenching reason. The PL intensity declined from 300,000 cps to 50,000 cps, suggesting that temperature fluctuations can induce fluorescence quenching, as shown in Fig. 3(a).

The effect of Fe3+ metal ions added to the carbon quantum dot solution on the fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. 3(b). The fluorescence intensity of the carbon quantum dot solution decreased after the addition of metal ions. The mechanism by which metal ions cause fluorescence quenching is attributed to static quenching (Jiang et al., 2019). This decrease in PL intensity showed that the Fe3+ metal ions and carbon quantum dots formed complexes, thus causing fluorescence quenching. The effect of the pH of the carbon quantum dot solution on the fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. 3(c). When the pH changed from 7 to 14, the fluorescence intensity of the carbon quantum dot solution increased. Because the surfaces of carbon quantum dots are rich in carboxyl, hydroxyl functional groups can be protonated and deprotonated. At high-pH values, the deprotonation of carboxyl groups changes the π electron cloud density distribution between the carbon backbone and the surface states (Sui et al., 2016). Therefore, electrons excited in a high-pH environment are likely to be captured on the surface within 1 ps, allowing the PL intensity to increase. In this study, PL spectroscopy was used to analyse the photostability and water stability of the carbon quantum dot solution, as shown in Fig. 3(d). The PL intensity of the solution decreased slightly but remained high after 7 days and the solution appeared transparent, which meant that the CD dispersed well in the water. Fig. 3(e) shows that adding carbon quantum dots to real food (carbonate beverages) caused fluorescence quenching. This was due to the presence of ponceau 4R in the food (carbonate beverages); this finding is the same as a previous result reference (Zhang et al., 2016).

Fig. 4 depicts the SEM images and EDX analysis results of the CD. The CD exhibited a spherical shape (red circles), had sizes of 3–10 nm and had a uniform size distribution (Fig. 4(a) and (b)). EDX analysis, which was used to determine the CD composition and estimate the abundance of carbon and oxygen present on them, showed that C and O were the dominant species (Fig. 4(c)). The element mapping diagram in (Fig. 4(d)) depicts evenly distributed carbon and oxygen throughout the carbon quantum dot structure. The element mapping results were the same as those of EDX, verifying the success of CD preparation.

A TEM image of the carbon quantum dots is shown in Fig. 5 . The small black spots in the image are the carbon quantum dot particles, which were spherical. The particles (sized approximately 3–10 nm) dispersed well, without any aggregation. Thus, the CD had good solubility in water.

The functional groups on the CD surface can be observed in the infrared (IR) analysis spectrum in Fig. 6 . In the broad band of 3021–3680 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of the OH functional group on the surface of the carbon quantum dots. The peak at 1640 cm−1 was ascribed to the COO− functional group.

The results of the XPS analysis of the carbon quantum dots are shown in Fig. 7 . The full-scan spectrum (Fig. 7(a)) shows that the carbon quantum dots had two element peaks: carbon (C1s peak at 284.5 eV) and oxygen (O1s peak at 531.5 eV). The three extended peaks of C1s (Fig. 7(b)) were the bands of CC (284.6 eV), CO (285.7 eV) and CO (288.1 eV). The two extended peaks of O1s (Fig. 7(c)) were the bands of CO (531.7.6 eV) and CO (532.8 eV). Thus, the surfaces of the carbon quantum dots contained two polar functional groups: hydroxyl and carboxyl. The XRD pattern of the carbon quantum dots is shown in Fig. 7(d). A broad peak was seen at 2θ = 20°–30°. The angle range in the figure shows that the synthesised carbon quantum dots had an amorphous structure.

Fig. 8 shows the changes in the brightness of the carbon quantum dot solution during temperature, metal ion and pH sensing, as evaluated using a commercially available luminance metre. Fig. 8(a) red cycle by using black cap to avoid another light in the metre sensing and set up as a blank to measure it brightness. After UV light irradiation, the brightness only slightly increased from 5 lx to 7 lx (red text in images). When the quantum dots sensed a temperature change from 25 °C to 100 °C, the brightness decreased from 47 lx to 25 lx (red text in images), as shown in Fig. 8(b). When temperature rises, the fluorescence intensity may decrease due to the aggregation of quantum dots; this phenomenon reduced the brightness, as shown in Fig. 8(b). After the addition of iron metal ions to the quantum dots, the luminescence value reduced from 47 lx to 2 lx, as shown in Fig. 8(c). After the sensed pH changed from 7 to 14, the luminescence value increased from 47 lx to 69 lx, as shown in Fig. 8(d). In the sensing of real food (carbonate beverages) using the quantum dots, the luminescence value reduced from 47 lx to 29 lx, as shown in Fig. 8(e). The UV light irradiation direction was perpendicular to the analysis direction of the luminance metre, as shown in Fig. 8(f).

In this study, a green, rapid method was developed for synthesising CD. This method, which does not involve toxic substances, proceeds through a cutting process based on NaCl crystals and utilises watermelon rind as the carbon source. The synthesised CD exhibited strong blue fluorescence under UV light, dispersed well in aqueous solutions and exhibited photostability. Thus, they are a promising candidate for sensor applications.

Jun Yi Wu: . Yi Chen Huang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) of Taiwan (MOST 111-2222-E-231-001-).
The authors thank the Electron Microscopy Center of Institute of Marine Biology at National Taiwan Ocean University for technical assistance.
